preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiced alveolar plosive /d/ to a close back rounded vowel /u/ is not a direct single-step shift, but rather a multi-stage process involving lenition and subsequent vocalization. This chain typically involves an intermediate stage where /d/ first lenites to a labial-velar approximant /w/, which then vocalizes to /u/.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (plosive to approximant), Vocalization (approximant to vowel), Labialization, Backing, Rounding

examples.note: Latin /d/ lenited to /w/ (represented as 'u' in orthography) in words like Latin 'sĕdēre' becoming Catalan 'seure'.
A shift from 'd'-forms to 'w'-forms was ongoing in Early and Late Modern Dutch, indicating a lenition of /d/ to /w/.
The change of /d/ to /w/ is attested in Mukrī Kurdish dialects.
Vocalization of [w] to [u] after Middle English vowels, forming diphthongs like [au] from 'strawe'.
Vocalization of [w] to [u] after Middle English vowels, forming diphthongs like [ou] from 'glowen'.
Vocalization of [w] to [u] after Middle English vowels, forming diphthongs like [eu] from 'fewe'.
Vocalization of [w] to [u] after Middle English vowels, forming diphthongs like [iu] from 'trewe' or 'newe'.
Vocalization of /w/ to /u/ is dated to the fifteenth century in Welsh.
A shift of consonantal /w/ to /u/ (or /o/) is attested, with the 'w' continuing to be written even after it ceased being pronounced as a consonant.

sources: Brinton, L. J., & Arnovick, L. K. (2024). The English Language: A Linguistic History. Z-Library.
